events.label: assistant
events.text:
Assessment complete — written to `/app/answer.json`.

**Decision: fail (high risk).** A backstabbed (push-in) termination on a 20A circuit is the key issue: those spring-clip terminals rely on friction contact rather than a screw-clamped connection, and commodity devices with backstab holes are typically listed only for 14 AWG/15A use. Using this termination style on a 20A (12 AWG) circuit installs the device outside its listing (NEC 110.3(B)/110.14(A)) and creates a real loosening/high-resistance/arcing risk — which is exactly why journeymen refuse to do it even where it isn't explicitly banned. Since the work is only rough-complete, corrective action (re-terminate on screw/clamp terminals or pigtail with a listed connector) should happen before cover-up or energizing.
